questions.text: Which of the following compounds will give a secondary alcohol after reaction with NaBH4?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Option A is a ketone, and its reduction (NaBH4 is a reducing agent) results in a secondary alcohol. This is because the carbonyl functional group is present in the middle of the chain so, the hydroxyl group is formed in the same place after reduction, giving a secondary alcohol.
Option B is an ester, which does not undergo reduction with NaBH.
Option C is an aldehyde, that forms a primary alcohol, upon reduction, as its carbonyl group is towards the terminal end of the chain, thereby forming a terminal hydroxyl group.
Option D is a carboxylic acid, which does not undergo reduction with NaBH4.

questions.explanation: Option A is a ketone, and its reduction (NaBH4 is a reducing agent) results in a secondary alcohol. This is because the carbonyl functional group is present in the middle of the chain so, the hydroxyl group is formed in the same place after reduction, giving a secondary alcohol.Option B is an ester, which does not undergo reduction with NaBH4.Option C is an aldehyde, that forms a primary alcohol, upon reduction, as its carbonyl group is towards the terminal end of the chain, thereby forming a terminal hydroxyl group.Option D is a carboxylic acid, which does not undergo reduction with NaBH4.

questions.text: Which of the following compounds is solid at room temperature?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: At room temperature ethanol and methanol are both liquid.
At room temperature ethanol and methanol are both liquid.
Butane is a gas at room temperature and atmospheric pressure.
Phenols occur as white solids at room temperature and may be highly toxic and caustic.

questions.explanation: Phenols occur as white solids at room temperature and may be highly toxic and caustic.

questions.text: The test used to distinguish among primary, secondary, and tertiary alcohols is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The 2,4-DNPH test is commonly used to detect the presence of carbonyl compounds, such as aldehydes and ketones, but it is not specific for distinguishing between primary, secondary, and tertiary alcohols.
The Tollens test is used to identify the presence of aldehydes in a sample.
The Lucas Test is used to distinguish between primary, secondary, and tertiary alcohols based on their reactivity with Lucas reagent (a mixture of concentrated hydrochloric acid and zinc chloride).
The Fehling solution test is primarily used to detect the presence of reducing sugars, such as aldehydes and alpha-hydroxy ketones.

questions.explanation: The Lucas test is used to differentiate primary, secondary, and tertiary alcohols. It is based on the rate of turbidity formation due to alkyl chloride formation. Tertiary alcohols react fastest, secondary slower, and primary show little or no reaction.

questions.text: Which of the following is TRUE regarding Methyl Alcohol?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: It is a colorless, volatile, thin liquid with specific gravity 0.796 at 15°C.
options.explanation: This is the correct option as it accurately describes the physical properties of methyl alcohol, also known as methanol.

options.text: It is used for low-temperature thermometers and as a fuel substitute.
options.explanation: These are characteristics of Ethanol, not Methyl Alcohol. Methyl Alcohol is toxic and primarily used as an industrial solvent and antifreeze.

options.text: It is extensively used in the formation of different beverages.
options.explanation: These are characteristics of Ethanol. Methyl Alcohol is toxic and not suitable for consumption.

options.text: It is used as a base for perfumes.
options.explanation: These are characteristics of esters. Methyl Alcohol is not used in perfumes due to its toxicity.

options.text: It is used as an antiseptic and disinfectant.
options.explanation: These are characteristics of Phenol. Methyl Alcohol is not used for sanitization purposes due to its toxicity.

questions.explanation: Methyl Alcohol, also known as methanol, is a colorless, volatile, and thin liquid with a specific gravity of 0.796 at 15°C. This makes Option A the correct choice. Methyl Alcohol is primarily used as an industrial solvent and antifreeze, and is not suitable for consumption or use in perfumes due to its toxicity. Options B and C describe Ethanol, which is used in thermometers and beverages. Option D describes esters, often used in perfumes. Option E describes Phenol, which is used as an antiseptic and disinfectant.

questions.text: Ethanol, when reacted with PCI5 , gave A, POCl3, and HCI. A reacts with AgNO2 to form B and AgCl. A and B are respectively:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because C2H5OC2H5 (diethyl ether) is not formed in the given reactions.
This option is incorrect as neither C2H6 (ethane) nor diethyl ether is produced from the reactions described.
This is the correct option. Ethanol reacts with PCl5 to produce C2H5Cl, which subsequently reacts with AgNO2 to form C2H5NO2.
C2H6 is not formed in this reaction sequence. The correct product from C2H5Cl and AgNO2 is C2H5NO2.
Neither C2H6 nor C2H6NO are products of the reactions given. This option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: The sequence of reactions begins with ethanol (C2H5OH) reacting with phosphorus pentachloride (PCl5), resulting in the formation of ethyl chloride (C2H5Cl), phosphorus oxychloride (POCl3), and hydrochloric acid (HCl). The ethyl chloride then reacts with silver nitrate (AgNO2), producing nitroethane (C2H5NO2) and silver chloride (AgCl). The correct products for A and B in the sequence are thus ethyl chloride and nitroethane, respectively. The other options involve incorrect products, such as diethyl ether, ethane, or incorrect formulas, and do not match the reaction steps provided.

questions.text: Dehydration of ethanol at 140 oC in the presence of concentrated H2SO4 gives:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: When the temperature of about 180° C then it will give an alkenes otherwise it will give an ether as an product.
Ethane is not a product of the dehydration of ethanol. Ethane is an alkane, meaning it has only single bonds between carbon atoms. It does not form directly from the dehydration process.

Ethyne, commonly known as acetylene, is also not a product of the dehydration of ethanol. Ethyne is an alkyne with a triple bond between the two carbon atoms. It does not form directly from the dehydration process.
When the temperature of about 180° C then it will give an alkenes otherwise it will give an ether as an product.

questions.text: Phenol reacts with CH3COCl to give:

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Phenol does not react with CH₃COCl to form an acid; rather, it reacts to form an ester.

This option is incorrect. The reaction does not yield an alcohol; instead, it forms an ester as phenol is acylated.
This option is incorrect. The reaction with CH₃COCl does not produce an aldehyde; it forms an ester instead.

questions.explanation: Phenol reacts with acetyl chloride (CH₃COCl) by acylation. The –OH group of phenol is converted into an ester linkage. This reaction forms phenyl acetate, which is an ester.

questions.text: The product formed by acid catalysed hydration of 2 - phenyl propene is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: How it is possible when phenyl group is at 2nd carbon , so it will remain in 2nd carbon.

The answer is B as we have the phenyl on the second carbon and the double bond being either between carbon number 1 and 2 or between carbon number 2 and 3. Based on the information about the position of the phenyl group, we can rule out options A, C, and D as all three show the phenyl having its position changed which is not possible. Hydration would remove the double bond, replacing it with a hydroxyl group that could be on either three of the carbons. As we only have option B satisfying our condition of the phenyl group being on the second carbon, we can infer the hydroxyl is on the second carbon as well based on the options provided.Also remember, the major product obtained on acid - catalyzed hydration of 2-phenylpropene is 2-Phenylpropan-2-ol.The major product obtained on acid - catalyzed hydration of 2-phenylpropene is 2-Phenylpropan-2-ol. questions.text: The compound ‘leaf alcohol’ is partly responsible for the smell of new - mown grass CH3CH2CH=CHCH2CH2OHLeaf alcoholWhich two compounds will be formed when ‘leaf alcohol’ is oxidized using hot, concentrated manganate (VII) ions?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Carboxylic group will be formed with both carbons which form double bond, Now just count carbon numbers in option, do they satisfy the fact that if we break from double bond ,two compounds will be formed having the same number of carbons they were having on both sides of double bond. This option doesn't satisfy that.
First acid should have 3 carbon as there are three carbons on left side of double bond. but in this option there are 2 carbons which is wrong .

When the double bond breaks, based on the structure of both the carbons involved in the double bond, each having a hydrogen attached alongside an organic chain, aldehydes would be formed. Both the aldehydes would be oxidized to give carboxylic acid. CH3CH2COOH is formed plus COOHCH2CH2OH with the latter being a primary alcohol. It would be further oxidized to give a dicarboxylic acid which is HOOCCH2COOH, thus the answer is C.
See second acid, OH group is attatched in place of OOH where there was double bond , so it is also wrong.

questions.explanation: The answer is C as when the double bond breaks, based on the structure of both the carbons involved in the double bond, each having a hydrogen attached alongside an organic chain, aldehydes would be formed. Both the aldehydes would be oxidized to give carboxylic acid. CH3CH2COOH is formed plus COOHCH2CH2OH with the latter being a primary alcohol. It would be further oxidized to give a dicarboxylic acid which is HOOCCH2COOH, thus the answer is C.

questions.text: Rectified spirits have a percentage of alcohol.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: 12% alcohol is the typical alcohol content of beer.
24% alcohol is the typical alcohol content of wine.

Rectified spirit has a percentage of alcohol of 95%. It is a type of ethanol that has been purified through a process called rectification. Rectification is a distillation process that separates ethanol from water. The ethanol is then concentrated to a high level of purity. Rectified spirit is often used as a solvent in industrial applications. It is also used in the production of alcoholic beverages, such as vodka and gin. However, it is not safe to drink rectified spirit because it can be toxic.
100% alcohol is pure ethanol.

questions.explanation: Option A: 12% alcohol is the typical alcohol content of beer. Option B: 24% alcohol is the typical alcohol content of wine. Option C: Rectified spirit has a percentage of alcohol of 95%. It is a type of ethanol that has been purified through a process called rectification. Rectification is a distillation process that separates ethanol from water. The ethanol is then concentrated to a high level of purity. Rectified spirit is often used as a solvent in industrial applications. It is also used in the production of alcoholic beverages, such as vodka and gin. However, it is not safe to drink rectified spirit because it can be toxic. Option D: 100% alcohol is pure ethanol.

questions.text: Which of the following is more soluble in water?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Ethanol (C₂H₅OH):Contains a hydroxyl (-OH) group, which can form strong hydrogen bonds with water.Has a short alkyl chain, making it highly soluble in water.
Phenol (C₆H₅OH):Has a hydroxyl (-OH) group, allowing hydrogen bonding.However, the benzene ring is hydrophobic, reducing its solubility compared to ethanol.
Hexanol (C₆H₁₃OH):Also has a hydroxyl (-OH) group but a longer nonpolar hydrocarbon chain.The increased hydrophobicity reduces solubility compared to ethanol.
Dimethyl ether (CH₃OCH₃):Has an oxygen atom but lacks an -OH group, meaning it cannot form strong hydrogen bonds with water.It is less soluble than ethanol.

questions.explanation: Ethanol is a small polar molecule, the OH group makes hydrogen bonding with water. As most higher alcohols are insoluble; hexanol is a long chain alcohol hence it is insoluble. Phenol and dimethyl ether are slightly soluble.

questions.text: Phenol is a(n) _ solid?

Phenol is a colorless, crystalline, deliquescent solid with a particular phenolic odor having melting point 41 ° C and boiling point 182 ° C.

questions.text: The alkenes can be formed by alcohols in the presence of

options.explanation: This reagent is primarily used for oxidation reactions and does not typically facilitate the dehydration of alcohols to form alkenes, making this option incorrect.

questions.explanation: Alcohols can be converted into alkenes through a dehydration reaction, which is often catalyzed by an acidic medium or a suitable reagent. The other options, such as acidified KMnO4, K2Cr2O7, and CuCl2, are primarily oxidizing agents and do not facilitate the dehydration process necessary for alkene formation. Therefore, they are not suitable reagents for this reaction.

questions.text: In the following reaction sequence, product D will be:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The above reaction scheme describes dehydrohalogenation of the alkyl halide into an alkene, followed by hydration of the alkene. The first step involves the reaction of propyl bromide with alcoholic KOH, which will result in elimination or dehydrohalogenation, whereby the halogen atom and adjacent carbon atom's hydrogen atom is removed, resulting in the formation of a double bond between the two carbons, forming propane, which is product C. Propene is acted upon by aqueous H2SO4, which implies hydration or addition of H2O, resulting in the formation of two products; the major product which is 2-propanol and the minor product which is 1-propanol so, the major product is the preferred option, i.e. option C.

Option B, ethanal is formed upon oxidation of the ethanol, which is formed.

questions.text: Which of the following reactions is used for the production of alcohols on industrial scale?

Option B describes a reaction that produces alcohol from alkenes so, it is correct.
Option C describes a reaction that produces alkyl halides from alkenes.
Option D describes a reaction that produces glycol from alkenes, which is a type of alcohol but not the type that is produced on an industrial scale for commercial use.

Option C describes a reaction that produces alkyl halides from alkenes. questions.text: How are carboxylic acids formed from alcohol?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Does Hydrolysis of Alcohols Occur? Technically, alcohols do not undergo "hydrolysis" in the usual sense.Hydrolysis is a reaction in which water breaks a bond in another molecule.Alcohols are already hydroxy compounds — so adding water doesn't break them further under normal conditions.

Reduction of Alcohols — ConceptReduction usually refers to gain of electrons or hydrogen or loss of oxygen.But since alcohols are already in a reduced state, reducing an alcohol further is possible only for specific types of alcohols, and it leads to alkanes.

Carboxylic acids can be formed from alcohols through a process called oxidation. The oxidation of alcohols involves the removal of hydrogen and addition of oxygen to the carbon atom that bears the hydroxyl group (-OH) in the alcohol molecule, resulting in the formation of a carboxylic acid functional group (-COOH).The specific method and reagents used for the oxidation of alcohols depend on the type of alcohol being oxidized. There are three main types of alcohols:1. Primary alcohols: These have the -OH group attached to a carbon atom that is bonded to only one other carbon atom.2. Secondary alcohols: These have the -OH group attached to a carbon atom that is bonded to two other carbon atoms.3. Tertiary alcohols: These have the -OH group attached to a carbon atom that is bonded to three other carbon atoms.The oxidation of primary alcohols to carboxylic acids can be achieved using various oxidizing agents, such as potassium permanganate (KMnO4), potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7), or Jones reagent (chromic acid and sulfuric acid). The reaction typically involves heating the alcohol with the oxidizing agent.The general reaction for the oxidation of a primary alcohol to a carboxylic acid is as follows:R-CH2-OH (primary alcohol) + [O] → R-COOH (carboxylic acid)For secondary alcohols, the oxidation can yield ketones instead of carboxylic acids, and for tertiary alcohols, oxidation reactions are not feasible under mild conditions to produce carboxylic acids or ketones. Therefore, primary alcohols are the most common starting materials for the synthesis of carboxylic acids through alcohol oxidation.

What is Protonation of Alcohols?Protonation means addition of a proton (H⁺) to a molecule.When an alcohol (R–OH) is protonated, the oxygen atom of the –OH group accepts a proton because oxygen has lone pairs and is nucleophilic.This turns the –OH group into a better leaving group (H₂O).

questions.explanation: Primary alcohols can be 'oxidized' to form aldehydes and carboxylic acids; secondary alcohols can be oxidized to give ketones. Tertiary alcohols, in contrast, cannot be oxidized without breaking the molecule's C–C bonds.

questions.text: Phenol reacts with calcium carbonate and evolve _?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Phenol does not react with calcium carbonate (CaCO3) to evolve any gas. Phenol is a weak acid, and while it can be neutralized by strong bases like sodium hydroxide, it is not strong enough to react with weak acids like carbonic acid, which is formed when calcium carbonate dissolves in water.

questions.text: Which of following is an example of electrophilic attack on alcohols?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This reaction represents the formation of an ester through an electrophilic attack of the alcohol (ethanol) on the electrophile (acetic acid), resulting in an ester and water.
This reaction is a redox reaction where sodium reacts with ethanol to produce sodium ethoxide and hydrogen gas, but it does not illustrate an electrophilic attack.
This reaction shows the nucleophilic alcohol (ethanol) reacting with the electrophile (HCl) to form an alkyl halide (ethyl chloride) and water, illustrating an electrophilic attack on the alcohol.
This option incorrectly states that both A and B are examples of electrophilic attacks. However, only Option C represents an electrophilic attack on alcohols.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option C: C2H5OH + HCl → C2H5Cl + H2O. In this reaction, ethanol acts as a nucleophile and attacks the electrophile, HCl, resulting in the formation of ethyl chloride and water. This is a classic example of electrophilic substitution where the alcohol is converted to an alkyl halide.Option A represents an esterification reaction, which is not a direct electrophilic attack on the alcohol. Option B is a reaction of alcohol with sodium, which is a redox reaction and does not involve electrophilic attack. Finally, Option D is incorrect because it suggests both A and B are correct, but they do not represent electrophilic attacks.

questions.text: In the Lucas test, tertiary alcohols form an oily layer of alkyl halide _.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: On heating is incorrect because heating is not required for the reaction to take place. The reaction occurs at room temperature.

The Lucas test in alcohols is a test to differentiate between primary, secondary, and tertiary alcohols. Tertiary alcohols react immediately with the Lucas reagent as evidenced by turbidity owing to the low solubility of the organic chloride in the aqueous mixture.

After 5-10 minutes is also incorrect because the reaction is very fast and occurs immediately. The turbidity or precipitation of the solution is typically observed within a few seconds to a minute after the addition of Lucas reagent.

After 20 minutes is also incorrect because the reaction is very fast and occurs immediately. The turbidity or precipitation of the solution is typically observed within a few seconds to a minute after the addition of the Lucas reagent.

questions.explanation: Option A: On heating is incorrect because heating is not required for the reaction to take place. The reaction occurs at room temperature. Option B: The Lucas test in alcohols is a test to differentiate between primary, secondary, and tertiary alcohols. Tertiary alcohols react immediately with the Lucas reagent as evidenced by turbidity owing to the low solubility of the organic chloride in the aqueous mixture. Option C: After 5-10 minutes is also incorrect because the reaction is very fast and occurs immediately. The turbidity or precipitation of the solution is typically observed within a few seconds to a minute after the addition of the Lucas reagent. Option D: After 20 minutes is also incorrect because the reaction is very fast and occurs immediately. The turbidity or precipitation of the solution is typically observed within a few seconds to a minute after the addition of the Lucas reagent.

questions.text: The biochemical process used in the synthesis of alcohol in the presence of yeast is called _.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Respiration is the biochemical process of breaking down glucose for ATP synthesis. But it is a general term. So we cannot choose this option, and it is the incorrect option
Photosynthesis is the biochemical process by which carbon dioxide and water are converted to glucose in the presence of chlorophyll and sunlight. Hence, this option is incorrect.

Fermentation is the biochemical process by which glucose is not completely oxidized due to a lack of oxygen and forms either alcohol or lactic acid. It perfectly complements the given statement and, hence, is the correct option.
Aerobic respiration is the biochemical process by which glucose is completely oxidized into ATP. Alcohol is not formed. Hence, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Fermentation is the process whereby molecules such as glucose are broken down anaerobically, producing alcohol.

questions.text: Why is phenol more acidic than Alcohol?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Due to the presence of resonance in phenol
options.explanation: It is true that resonance makes phenol more acidic as it allows delocalization of the negative charge. Hence, this statement is correct, just as option B is. So we cannot mark it as correct.

options.text: Due to the absence of resonance in alcohol
options.explanation: Alcohols do not have resonance in them because there is no conjugation and no ring. This is why alcohols are less acidic than phenols. Hence, this statement is also correct. But we cannot mark it correct because option A is also true.

options.text: Both Options A and B are correct
options.explanation: As both options A and B are correct, this option will be marked correct.

options.text: Because in phenol, hydrogen is attached to oxygen,
options.explanation: This is not a valid reason, and it does not affect the acidity of phenol. So this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Phenol is more acidic than alcohol due to the stabilization of the phenoxide ion through resonance. The presence of an electron-withdrawing group increases the acidity of phenol by stabilizing the phenoxide ion.

questions.text: The alcohols which are resistant to oxidation reactions are:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Primary alcohols can easily be oxidized; hence, this option is incorrect.
Secondary alcohols can also be oxidized with ease, although it is not as easy as with primary alcohols. Hence, this option is also wrong.

In tertiary alcohols, the alpha carbon is attached to three more carbons. Two hydrogen atoms cannot be lost from the alpha carbon, which is necessary for the oxidation of alcohols. Hence, this option is the correct one.

options.text: All of above options are correct
options.explanation: As only option C is correct, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Tertiary alcohols because the carbon atom that carries the OH group does not have a hydrogen atom attached but is instead bonded to other carbon atoms, so two hydrogen atoms cannot be lost, which is necessary for oxidation.

questions.text: Alcohol in which carbon atom bonded to OH group is further attached with three alkyl group is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: A tertiary (3°) alcohol is one in which the carbon atom with the OH group is attached to three other carbon atoms. Its general formula is R 3COH
Primary alcohols are those alcohols where the carbon atom of the hydroxyl group (OH) is attached to only one single alkyl group.
The aromatic alcohols or aryl- alcohols are a class of chemical compounds containing a hydroxyl group (-OH) bonded directly to an aromatic hydrocarbon group.
A secondary alcohol is a compound in which a hydroxyl group(-OH) is attached to a saturated carbon atom which has two other carbon atoms attached to it.

questions.explanation: A tertiary (3°) alcohol is one in which the carbon atom with the OH group is attached to three other carbon atoms. Its general formula is R3COHPrimary alcohols are those alcohols where the carbon atom of the hydroxyl group (OH) is attached to only one single alkyl group.The aromatic alcohols or aryl-alcohols are a class of chemical compounds containing a hydroxyl group (—OH) bonded directly to an aromatic hydrocarbon group.A secondary alcohol is a compound in which a hydroxyl group( ‒OH) is attached to a saturated carbon atom which has two other carbon atoms attached to it.

questions.text: Why is it necessary to distill aldehyde formed from oxidation of primary alcohol through acidified potassium dichromate (VI) solution or acidified sodium dichromate (VI) solution?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Aldehyde formed may be oxidized further to carboxylic acid concerned
options.explanation: Oxidation of alcohols to aldehydes is partial oxidation, if aldehydes are further oxidized they form carboxylic acids. Therefore, the conditions required for making aldehydes are heat and distillation.

options.text: Aldehyde formed is unstable and decompose back to original precursor i.e. primary alcohol
options.explanation: Oxidation of alcohols to aldehydes is partial oxidation, if aldehydes are further oxidized they form carboxylic acids. Therefore, the conditions required for making aldehydes are heat and distillation.

options.text: Aldehyde may be oxidised further to a ketone
options.explanation: Oxidation of alcohols to aldehydes is partial oxidation, if aldehydes are further oxidized they form carboxylic acids. Therefore, the conditions required for making aldehydes are heat and distillation.

options.text: Aldehyde formed may react with primary alcohol, the original reactant.
options.explanation: Oxidation of alcohols to aldehydes is partial oxidation, if aldehydes are further oxidized they form carboxylic acids. Therefore, the conditions required for making aldehydes are heat and distillation.

questions.explanation: Aldehyde formed may be oxidized further to carboxylic acid Primary alcohols oxidized with acidified K2 Cr2O7/Na2Cr2O7 can form aldehydes. Aldehydes are easily further oxidized to carboxylic acids in the same reaction mixture. Distillation removes the aldehyde immediately, preventing over- oxidation.

questions.text: Which one of the following compounds is known as tertiary alcohol?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This is primary alcohol.
2-methyl-2-propanol / tert-Butyl alcohol, also called tert-butanol or t-butanol, is the simplest tertiary alcohol, with a formula of (CH3)2COH.
Isobutanol, also known as iso-butyl alcohol or 2-methylpropanol, belongs to the class of organic compounds known as primary alcohols.
2-Propanol, or isopropyl alcohol, is a three-carbon alcohol with the OH group on the middle carbon. Isopropyl alcohol is a secondary alcohol

questions.explanation: 2-methyl-2-propanol / tert-Butyl alcohol, also called tert-butanol or t-butanol, is the simplest tertiary alcohol, with a formula of (CH₃)₃COH.

questions.text: Glycerol can also be termed as?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: 1 - butanol: Incorrect because 1-butanol is a four-carbon straight-chain alcohol, while glycerol (also known as glycerin or glycerine) is a three-carbon compound, making them different chemical entities.

Glycerol contains three carbons, giving it the “propane” part to its name. It has an OH group attached to each carbon which account for “1,2,3- triol” Hence, option B is correct as it has 3 hydroxyl groups.
2 - methyl - propanol: Incorrect because 2-methyl-propanol is an isomer of butanol and not related to glycerol in terms of its chemical structure and properties.

Isobutyl alcohol: Incorrect because isobutyl alcohol is also an isomer of butanol and has a different chemical structure from glycerol, which is a trihydroxy alcohol with three hydroxyl groups on a three-carbon chain.

questions.explanation: Glycerol contains three carbons, giving it the “propane” part to its name. It has an OH group attached to each carbon which account for “1,2,3- triol” Hence, option B is correct as it has 3 hydroxyl groups.

questions.text: What is the other name of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The other name of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol is picric acid. It is a yellow, crystalline solid that is highly explosive. It is also a strong acid and can cause severe burns. The name "picric" comes from the Greek word "pikros", which means "bitter". This is because picric acid has a very bitter taste. Picric acid was first synthesized in 1771 by Carl Wilhelm Scheele. It was originally used as a yellow dye, but it was later found to be too explosive for this purpose. Today, picric acid is used mainly as an explosive. It is also used in some fireworks and as a component of some smokeless powders.
Nitrophenol is a general term for any phenol that has been nitrated.
TNT is a specific compound that is made up of 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene.
Benzophenone is a different compound altogether. It is a colorless, crystalline solid that is used in perfumes and plastics.

questions.explanation: Correct option is A. The other name of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol is picric acid. It is a yellow, crystalline solid that is highly explosive. It is also a strong acid and can cause severe burns. The name "picric" comes from the Greek word "pikros", which means "bitter". This is because picric acid has a very bitter taste. Picric acid was first synthesized in 1771 by Carl Wilhelm Scheele. It was originally used as a yellow dye, but it was later found to be too explosive for this purpose. Today, picric acid is used mainly as an explosive. It is also used in some fireworks and as a component of some smokeless powders.

questions.text: Dehydration of alcohols at low temperature and high acid concentration results in?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Alcohol can be dehydrated to form either alkene ( higher temperature and excess acid) or ethers ( low temperature and excess acid).
Alcohol can be dehydrated to form either alkene ( higher temperature and excess acid) or ethers ( low temperature and excess acid).
This option is incorrect because dehydration of alcohols don't produce carboxylic acid.
This option is incorrect because dehydration of alcohols don't produce aldehydes.

questions.explanation: Correct option is B.Alcohols on dehydration can produce ethers as well as alkene under different conditions but mainly ether.Alcohol can be dehydrated to form either alkene ( higher temperature and excess acid) or ethers ( low temperature and excess acid).Dehydration of alcohol proceeds through the formation of carbonium ions. The carbonium ion can lose a proton to form alkene. The carbonium ion may be attacked by a fresh molecule of alcohol and form an oxonium ion which can then lose a proton to form ether.Usually, secondary and tertiary alcohols form alkene.

questions.text: NaBH4 causes reduction of aldehyde and ketones into _?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option is correct because Aldehyde and ketones are reduced by sodium borohydride and lithium aluminum hydride which transforms aldehydes and ketones to the corresponding alcohol, primary or secondary, respectively.
This option is incorrect because aldehyde and ketones on reduction are not converted into alkenes, phenols and alkanes.
This option is incorrect because aldehyde and ketones on reduction are not converted into alkenes, phenols and alkanes.
This option is incorrect because aldehyde and ketones on reduction are not converted into alkenes, phenols and alkanes.

questions.explanation: Correct option is A. Aldehyde and ketones are reduced by sodium borohydride and lithium aluminum hydride. Sodium borohydride (NaBH4) is a reagent that transforms aldehydes and ketones to the corresponding alcohol, primary or secondary, respectively.

questions.text: When an electrophilic reagent attacks on alcohol, _.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because when electrophile or nucleophile attacks there is no bond formation.
This option is correct because When electrophile attacks on alcohol O-H bonds break. The reactivity order of alcohols is 1∘>2∘>3∘ for the reaction in which O-H bond is broken .
This option is incorrect because when nucleophile attacks on alcohol C-O bonds break.
This option is incorrect because it do not raise its boiling point.

questions.explanation: Correct option is B. Alcohols react both as nucleophiles and electrophiles. O-H bond cleavage takes place when alcohols react asnucleophiles. And C-O bond cleavage takes place when they react as electrophiles. So, this is the basis of cleavage type in alcohols When electrophile attacks on alcohol O-H bonds break.

questions.text: Which one of the following shows that iodoform test for a compound is positive?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This is not correct. The formation of a carboxylate salt typically occurs when carboxylic acids react with bases, which is unrelated to the iodoform test.
This is not correct. A brick red precipitate is a result of Fehling's test, which is used for detecting aldehydes, not for the iodoform test.

This is correct. The iodoform test yields yellow crystals of iodoform (CHI₃), indicating a positive result. These yellow crystals have a distinct medicinal odor and are formed when a compound with a methyl ketone or a secondary alcohol oxidizable to a methyl ketone reacts with iodine in the presence of a base.
This is not correct. While water can be a byproduct in various reactions, its formation does not specifically indicate a positive iodoform test.

questions.explanation: The positive result of the iodoform test is indicated by the formation of yellow crystals. These crystals are iodoform (CHI₃), a pale yellow solid with a characteristic medicinal odor. Here's a breakdown:Iodoform Test: This test is used to detect the presence of a carbonyl group (C=O) with a methyl group directly attached to it (CH₃CO-) or an alcohol that can be oxidized to such a carbonyl group (CH₃CH(OH)-). Positive Result: When iodine (I₂) and a base (such as NaOH) are added to a compound containing these groups, a reaction occurs that produces iodoform (CHI₃). This iodoform precipitates out of the solution as distinct yellow crystals. The other options are incorrect:Formation of carboxylate salt: This is a general characteristic of carboxylic acids reacting with bases, but not specific to the iodoform test.Brick red precipitate formation: This is characteristic of Fehling's test, which is used to detect aldehydes.Formation of water: Water is often a byproduct of many organic reactions, but its formation is not the indicator of a positive iodoform test.Therefore, the correct answer is Yellow crystals.

questions.text: Which of the following forms primary alcohol?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.explanation: Ethyl acetate, upon hydrolysis by mineral acid, gives ethanol and acetic acid. Ethanol is a primary alcohol.

questions.text: This is used as a substrate during the formation of alcohol:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Sucrose is transformed into glucose and fructose by the enzyme invertase during fermentation, which are then converted into alcohol. It is a common substrate used in alcohol production.
Although glucose is directly used in fermentation, it is not the initial substrate for many industrial alcohol fermentations where sucrose is preferred due to its availability and cost-effectiveness.
Galactose is not typically used as a substrate in conventional alcohol production processes, as it is less common and not as economically viable as sucrose.
Mannose is rarely used as a substrate in alcohol fermentation because it is not as readily available or as efficient in fermentation processes compared to other sugars like sucrose.

questions.explanation: Sucrose or cane sugar is widely distributed among higher plants. Its commercial source is solely sugarcane and beet. It is used as a substrate for the formation of alcohol. Glucose is a widely distributed sugar.

questions.text: 1-Propanol reacts with HCl in the presence of ZnCl₂ _?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because 1-Propanol does react with HCl in the presence of ZnCl₂, but under specific conditions.
Correct. 1-Propanol requires heating to react in the Lucas test due to its primary alcohol structure.
Incorrect. Primary alcohols like 1-Propanol do not react immediately in the Lucas test without heating.
Incorrect. The reaction time for primary alcohols is not typically specified as a fixed duration but depends on heating.

questions.explanation: This is the reaction indicating Lucas Test which uses Lucas Reagent(ZnCl2+conc.HCl).Since 1-Propanol is a primary alcohol, it reacts with HCl in the presence of ZnCl₂ on heating, because it needs high energy to undergo the reaction, and results in formation of an insoluble primary alkyl chloride.

questions.text: Ethanol reacts with which of the following reagent to form ethyl chloride?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option is incorrect.H₂SO₄ (Sulfuric Acid):Sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄) does not directly produce ethyl chloride.

This option is incorrect.NaOH (Sodium Hydroxide):NaOH is a strong base, but it doesn't directly form ethyl chloride from ethanol. Instead, NaOH could deprotonate ethanol in a reaction, but it wouldn’t introduce chlorine to form the chloride.

Ethanol can react with PCl3 (phosphorus trichloride) to form ethyl chloride (ethyl alcohol). This reaction is known as the Fischer esterification reaction. The reaction proceeds by the substitution of the hydroxyl group (-OH) in ethanol with the chloride group (-Cl) from PCl3.The reaction can be represented as follows:Ethanol + PCl3 → Ethyl chloride + H3PO3

options.text: All of these options are correct
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Only option C is correct.

questions.explanation: Ethanol can react with PCl3 (phosphorus trichloride) to form ethyl chloride (ethyl alcohol). This reaction is known as the Fischer esterification reaction. The reaction proceeds by the substitution of the hydroxyl group (-OH) in ethanol with the chloride group (-Cl) from PCl3.The reaction can be represented as follows:Ethanol + PCl3 → Ethyl chloride + H3PO3

questions.text: The strongest acid among the following is?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Carbolic acid is a weak organic acid with a pKa of around 15. It is a slightly acidic compound and is slightly more acidic than water.

Water is a neutral compound with a pH of 7, but it can act as a weak acid in certain solutions. This is due to the presence of Hydronium ions (H3O+) that form when water dissolves in acidic solutions.
Methanol is a weak organic acid with a pKa of around 15. It is a weaker acid than water and phenol.
Butanoic acid is a stronger organic acid with a pKa of around 4.5. It is a stronger acid than water, phenol, and methanol.

questions.explanation: OPTION A: Carbolic acid is a weak organic acid with a pKa of around 10. It is a slightly acidic compound and is a weaker acid than water.OPTION B: Water is a neutral compound with a pH of 7, but it can act as a weak acid in certain solutions. This is due to the presence of Hydronium ions (H3O+) that form when water dissolves in acidic solutions.OPTION C: Methanol is a weak organic acid with a pKa of around 15. It is a weaker acid than water and phenol.OPTION D: Butanoic acid is a stronger organic acid with a pKa of around 4.5. It is a stronger acid than water, phenol, and methanol.

questions.text: The strength of an acid depends on?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Ease of removal of a proton: The more readily an acid can donate a proton, the stronger it is. Acids with weak bonds between hydrogen and the rest of the molecule are more likely to lose the proton and, thus, are stronger acids.

Stability of the anion formed: After the acid donates its proton, the resulting anion (conjugate base) is formed. A stable anion is better able to handle the negative charge that results from gaining an electron. An unstable or highly reactive anion will make the acid weaker.
Stability is not created by cation formation.

options.text: Depends on both ease of removal of proton and stability of the anion formed
options.explanation: The strength of an acid depends on both the ease of removal of a proton and the stability of the anion formed after the proton donation.

questions.explanation: The correct option is: Depends on both ease of removal of a proton and stability of the anion formed.Explanation:The strength of an acid is determined by its ability to donate a proton (H+) in a chemical reaction. This is often represented by the equation:Acid (AH) ⇌ H+ + Anion (A-)In this equation, the acid (AH) donates a proton (H+) to form the conjugate base or anion (A-). The strength of an acid is influenced by two main factors:Ease of removal of a proton: The more readily an acid can donate a proton, the stronger it is. Acids with weak bonds between hydrogen and the rest of the molecule are more likely to lose the proton and, thus, are stronger acids.Stability of the anion formed: After the acid donates its proton, the resulting anion (conjugate base) is formed. A stable anion is better able to handle the negative charge that results from gaining an electron. An unstable or highly reactive anion will make the acid weaker.Together, these factors play a crucial role in determining the strength of an acid. Acids with both a high tendency to release protons (ease of removal of a proton) and produce stable anions are considered strong acids. On the other hand, weak acids have a lower tendency to donate protons and/or form less stable anions.In summary, the strength of an acid depends on both the ease of removal of a proton and the stability of the anion formed after the proton donation.

questions.text: Which of the following is a strong acid?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Ethane is an alkane, and has no acidic properties. Hence, this option is incorrect.
Ethyl chloride is an alkyl halide. It also has no acidic properties, hence, this option is also incorrect.
Ethanol is an alcohol. It is a weak acid, as it dissociates to form ethoxy ion and hydrogen ion. But due to it being a weak acid, this option is incorrect.

Phenol is an aromatic compound that has a hydroxyl group attached to a benzene ring. It dissociates into hydrogen ion and phenoxide ion. Due to resonance, phenoxide ion is very stable, making phenol a strong acid. Hence, this option is the correct option.

questions.explanation: Phenols are much more acidic than alcohols because the negative charge in the phenoxide ion is not localized on the oxygen atom, as it is in an alkoxide ion, but is delocalized-it is shared by a number of carbon atoms in the benzene ring. On ther other hand, ethane and ethyl chloride doesn't display acidic behavior. Thus, option D is correct.

questions.text: Phenol is also called _.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Carbonic acid is a carbon-containing compound which has the chemical formula H2CO3. Solutions of carbon dioxide in water contain small amounts of this compound.

Phenol is an aromatic compound. The chemical formula of this organic compound is C6H6O. Phenol is also known as Carbolic acid. It consists of a hydroxyl group and a phenyl group attached to each other.

Acetic acid is also known as ethanoic acid, ethylic acid, vinegar acid, and methane carboxylic acid; it has the chemical formula of CH3COOH. Acetic acid is a byproduct of fermentation, and gives vinegar its characteristic odor.

Hydroxy acids, also known as polycarboxylic acids, has two carboxylic groups, at least. They can also present one carboxylic group or a hydroxyl group with a ketone. The most common hydroxy acids used to make multicomponent complexes with cyclodextrin are: citric acid, tartaric acid, glycolic acid, oxalic acid.

questions.explanation: Option A: Carbonic acid is a carbon-containing compound that has the chemical formula H2CO3. Solutions of carbon dioxide in water contain small amounts of this compound.Option B: Phenol is an aromatic compound. The chemical formula of this organic compound is C6H6O. Phenol is also known as Carbolic acid. It consists of a hydroxyl group and a phenyl group attached to each other.Option C: Acetic acid is also known as ethanoic acid, ethylic acid, vinegar acid, and methane carboxylic acid; it has the chemical formula of CH3COOH. Acetic acid is a by-product of fermentation and gives vinegar its characteristic odor.Option D: Hydroxy acids, also known as poly carboxylic acids, has two carboxylic groups, at least. They can also present one carboxylic group or a hydroxyl group with a ketone. The most common hydroxy acids used to make multicomponent complexes with cyclodextrin are: citric acid, tartaric acid, glycolic acid, and oxalic acid.

questions.text: Which of the following compound shows more hydrogen bonding?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Hyderogen bonding is more prominent in lower alcohols [mainly aliphatic alcohols]. Among the given options, methanol [CH3OH] is the lowest aliphatic alcohol so it shows maximum hydrogen bonding
It's shows less hydrogen bonding.
It's shows low hydrogen bonding due to benzene ring.
It's shows less hydrogen due to large R groups attached.

questions.explanation: Option A is correct as hydrogen bonding is prominent In lower alcohols such as methanol

questions.text: Which of the following alcohols does not convert into carboxylic acid when it reacts with K2Cr207/H2SO4?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Option A is tertiary alcoholsTertiary alcohols have the general structure R3COH, where R represents alkyl groups. In these alcohols, the carbon atom bearing the hydroxyl group is bonded to three other alkyl groups. Due to the absence of a hydrogen atom attached to the carbon bearing the hydroxyl group, the oxidation process cannot occur. So it will not produce carboxylic acid.

The alcohol CH3CH(CH3)OH is a secondary alcohol. Secondary alcohols can undergo oxidation when reacted with K2Cr2O7/H2SO4 and are converted into corresponding ketones.In the case of CH3CH(CH3)OH, it would be oxidized to CH3C(O)CH3 (acetone) when reacted with K2Cr2O7/H2SO4. It can further react in some cases to give carboxylic acid
Both option A and B is incorrect

The alcohol C2H5OH is ethanol, which is a primary alcohol. Primary alcohols can undergo oxidation when reacted with K2Cr2O7/H2SO4 and are converted into carboxylic acids.When C2H5OH reacts with K2Cr2O7/H2SO4, it would be oxidized to CH3COOH (acetic acid), which is a carboxylic acid.

questions.explanation: Tertiary alcohols do not undergo oxidation to produce carboxylic acids.

questions.text: Methanol is also called as?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Liquor is not the correct term for methanol.
Grain alcohol is also not a correct term for methanol.
Methanol is also called wood spirit, as it can be obtained by the destructive distillation of wood.
This option is also incorrect. Methanol is also used as a fuel in certain types of engines, but it is not typically referred to as "fuel" in the same way as gasoline or diesel.

questions.explanation: Methanol has the formula CH2OH. It is known as wood alcohol because it was once produced by the destructive distillation ( decomposition of wood at high temperatures without the presence of air) of wood.

questions.text: Which of the following is applicable for phenols?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Phenols are not always colorful crystalline solids. While some Phenols may be crystalline, others may be oily liquids or even gases at room temperature.

Phenols are generally less reactive to nucleophilic attack than other hydroxyl-containing compounds, such as alcohols, due to the presence of the aromatic ring, which stabilizes the Phenoxide anion formed during nucleophilic substitution reactions.

options.text: form link solution at room temperature
options.explanation: Phenols typically don't form a clear solution in water at room temperature. They are generally sparingly soluble in water and form an opaque or milky solution due to the formation of colloidal suspensions.
The OH group of phenol determines its properties. The OH group makes the phenol acidic and hence the acidic property allows it to dissolve readily in alkalines

questions.explanation: The OH group of phenol determines its properties. The OH group makes the phenol acidic and hence the acidic property allows it to dissolve readily in alkalines.

questions.text: The solubility of alcohols is due to?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Dipole moment is a measure of the polarity of a molecule, but it is not the primary reason for the solubility of alcohols.
Covalent bonds are the bonds that hold the atoms together within the molecule, and they don't directly contribute to the solubility of alcohols.

Alcohols have a hydroxyl (-OH) group, which can form hydrogen bonds with other polar molecules such as water. Hydrogen bonding is a strong intermolecular force that can lead to the solubility of alcohol in water and other polar solvents

Electronegativity is a measure of an atom's ability to attract electrons in a Covalent bond, and while it can contribute to the polarity of a molecule, it is not the primary reason for the solubility of alcohol.

questions.explanation: Alcohols have OH in which O has a lone pair hence can easily form hydrogen bonds with the H of water molecules. It dissolves in a polar solute by forming hydrogen bonds.

questions.text: Phenol is stronger acid than?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Carboxylic acids: Incorrect. Carboxylic acids (pKa ≈ 4-5) are much stronger acids than phenol due to the greater resonance stabilization of the carboxylate ion.

When a molecule of phenol loses a proton, it forms phenoxide ion which is stabilized by resonance as the negative charge is delocalized over the aromatic ring.No such resonance is present when a water molecule loses a proton to form hydroxide ion.Hence, phenol is more acidic than water.
This is not true as per the Explanation.Acetic acid: Incorrect. Acetic acid (pKa ≈ 4.76) is a stronger acid than phenol (pKa ≈ 9.95), meaning phenol is weaker in comparison.

options.text: None of these options are correct
options.explanation: None of these options are correct: Incorrect. Phenol is indeed a stronger acid than some substances, particularly water.

questions.explanation: Phenol dissociates more than water in an aqueous solution giving off H+ ions forming phenoxide. Acids are those that dissociate in an aqueous solution to give H+ ions.

questions.text: Which of following has "higher boiling"and "melting point"?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Acetone has a boiling point of 56.5°C and a melting point of -94.9°C. It is a relatively small molecule and lacks the ability to form hydrogen bonds, resulting in weaker intermolecular forces.

2-butanol has a higher molecular size and the presence of an -OH group, allowing for intermolecular hydrogen bonding. Hydrogen bonding leads to stronger intermolecular forces, resulting in higher boiling and melting points. 2-butanol has a boiling point of 99.5°C and a melting point of -114.5°C.Hence, B is the correct option
Propane (C3H8) is a small molecule with weak London dispersion forces between its nonpolar molecules. It has a boiling point of -42.1°C and a melting point of -187.7°C.
2-methyl propene (also known as isobutylene) (C4H8) is also a small molecule with weak intermolecular forces. It has a boiling point of -6.9°C and a melting point of -159.7°C

questions.explanation: OPTION A: Acetone has a boiling point of 56.5°C and a melting point of -94.9°C. It is a relatively small molecule and lacks the ability to form hydrogen bonds, resulting in weaker intermolecular forces.OPTION B: 2-butanol has a higher molecular size and the presence of an -OH group, allowing for intermolecular hydrogen bonding. Hydrogen bonding leads to stronger intermolecular forces, resulting in higher boiling and melting points. 2-butanol has a boiling point of 99.5°C and a melting point of -114.5°C.Hence, B is the correct option.OPTION C: Propane (C3H8) is a small molecule with weak London dispersion forces between its nonpolar molecules. It has a boiling point of -42.1°C and a melting point of -187.7°C.OPTION D: 2-methyl propene (also known as isobutylene) (C4H8) is also a small molecule with weak intermolecular forces. It has a boiling point of -6.9°C and a melting point of -159.7°C.

questions.text: Which of the following is an example of "tertiary alcohols"?

options.explanation: This is not even an alcohol, as there is no -OH group in the name.
Tertiary alcohols feature a hydroxyl group attached to the carbon atom, which is connected to 3- alkyl groups. 3-ethyl-3-hexanolHence, B is the correct option.
The -OH group is attached to C-4, which is bonded to only two carbon atoms (making it a secondary alcohol).
The -OH group is on a carbon that is attached to only two other carbon atoms (secondary alcohol).

questions.explanation: Tertiary alcohols feature a hydroxyl group attached to the carbon atom, which is connected to 3- 3-alkyl groups. 3-ethyl-3-hexanolHence, B is the correct option.The hydroxyl carbon has three R groups.
